---
title: "Black-eyed Susan (Rudbeckia hirta)"
slug: "rudbeckia-hirta"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/355243764/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Black-eyed Susan"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/355243764/medium.jpeg" alt="Black-eyed Susan" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/seandonlon2888">
seandonlon2888</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="http://inaturalist.org/observations/201259236">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=62741)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in fields, roadsides, and prairies.

## Natural history notes

A showy wildflower characterized by its bright yellow ray petals surrounding a dark brown to black central disk. In Florida, it is found in open habitats such as fields, roadsides, and prairies, often in sandy or well-drained soils. It typically blooms from late spring through summer and provides an important nectar source for bees, butterflies, and other pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/62741-Rudbeckia-hirta)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-rudbeckia-hirta/)
